coredata.dc:title: Band alignment driven efficient electron transfer and enhanced photoelectric property of gold nanoclusters sensitized graphene quantum dot composites
coredata.dc:creator: Hou, Guangjing
Pan, Yatao
Qiao, Lulu
Ran, Xia
Li, Tianfeng
Chi, Zhen
Liu, Pingan
He, Yulu
Kuang, Yanmin
Guo, Lijun
coredata.dc:description: Gold nanoclusters (Au NCs) have received much attention due to their promising applications in many fields including optical imaging, catalysis, biomedical science and, more recently, energy conversion. Herein, we have constructed a model composite of Au NCs and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and investigated the band alignment of their energetic levels, efficient electron transfer, and enhanced photoelectric properties of Au NCs/GQDs composites. The introduced Au NCs not only make up for the deficiency of light absorption of GQDs in the visible light region but importantly function as the electron donor to sensitize GQDs and improve the photoelectric properties of the composite. The results from steady-state and time-resolved measurements demonstrate a dramatically quenched fluorescence and a shortened lifetime of Au NCs in the composites, owing to the efficient electron transfer from Au NCs to GQDs. The electron transfer efficiency determined from the dynamical analysis is consistent with that of steady-state fluorescence quenching. The type-II band alignment of Au NCs and GQDs moieties effectively promotes the charge separation of excited Au NCs moieties and the electron transfer to GQDs. As a result, the photoelectric current of Au NCs/GQDs composite is enhanced by 2.69 and 4.80 times that of individual Au NCs and GQDs, respectively. These findings demonstrate a feasible strategy for constructing photoelectric composites with Au NCs as the sensitizer and carbon-based QDs as the electron acceptor and also provide photophysical insights into the structure-property relationship of Au NCs-based composites.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold nanocluster
Graphene quantum dot
Band alignment
Time-resolved spectroscopy
Photocurrent

The Science and Technology Development Project of Henan Province ( 222102210217 ). The Key Scientific Research Project of Colleges and Universities in Henan Province (Project no. 23A510013 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and HRTEM images of Au NCs (a) and GQDs (b). (c) Size distribution histograms of Au NCs (black) and GQDs (red). (d) UV–visible absorption (solid blue) and steady-state fluorescence (dashed blue) spectra of Au NCs under 470 nm excitation; absorption (0.3 mg/mL, solid black) and emission (dashed black) spectra of GQDs under 380 nm excitation.
(a) Tauc plots for the bandgap energies of Au NCs and GQDs. (b) The valence band XPS spectra of Au NCs and GQDs. (c) Schematic energetic diagram of the relative band levels for Au NCs and GQDs.

(a) Steady-state absorption and (b) fluorescence spectra of Au NCs with increasing the concentration of GQDs. The up and down arrows indicate the change tendencies of absorbance and fluorescence, respectively. (c) Fluorescence decay curves of Au NCs at 530 nm under 470 nm excitation and (d) GQDs at 460 nm under 380 nm excitation in different Au NCs/GQDs composites.

(a) Top-view of TA image of Au NCs/GQDs (0.3 mg/mL) under the pump at 470 nm. (b), (c) Spectral evolutions with the delay time of Au NCs and Au NCs/GQDs (0.3 mg/mL). The up arrows indicate the change tendencies of TA signals with increasing the delay time. (d) Kinetic traces around 513 nm of Au NCs and Au NCs/GQDs.

Photoelectric properties of Au NCs, GQDs and Au NCs/GQDs composite (0.3 mg/mL). (a) Schematic diagram of three-electrode electrochemical system; (b) LSV curves; (c) Current responses and (d) EIS curves.
The fitting parameters of fluorescence decay curves of Au NCs/GQDs with different contents of GQDs under the excitation at 470 nm.
Fitting parameters of the GSB decay curves at 513 nm for Au NCs/GQDs composites with different amounts of GQDs.

head.ce:title: Band alignment driven efficient electron transfer and enhanced photoelectric property of gold nanoclusters sensitized graphene quantum dot composites

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Supervision, Project administration, Writing − review & editing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Electronics, Academy for Advanced Interdisciplinary Studies, International Joint Research Laboratory of New Energy Materials and Devices of Henan Province, Henan University, Kaifeng 475004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Electronics, Academy for Advanced Interdisciplinary Studies, International Joint Research Laboratory of New Energy Materials and Devices of Henan Province, Henan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics and Electronics, Academy for Advanced Interdisciplinary Studies, International Joint Research Laboratory of New Energy Materials and Devices of Henan Province, Henan University, Kaifeng 475004, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Guangjing Hou and Yatao Pan contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Gold nanoclusters (Au NCs) have received much attention due to their promising applications in many fields including optical imaging, catalysis, biomedical science and, more recently, energy conversion. Herein, we have constructed a model composite of Au NCs and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and investigated the band alignment of their energetic levels, efficient electron transfer, and enhanced photoelectric properties of Au NCs/GQDs composites. The introduced Au NCs not only make up for the deficiency of light absorption of GQDs in the visible light region but importantly function as the electron donor to sensitize GQDs and improve the photoelectric properties of the composite. The results from steady-state and time-resolved measurements demonstrate a dramatically quenched fluorescence and a shortened lifetime of Au NCs in the composites, owing to the efficient electron transfer from Au NCs to GQDs. The electron transfer efficiency determined from the dynamical analysis is consistent with that of steady-state fluorescence quenching. The type-II band alignment of Au NCs and GQDs moieties effectively promotes the charge separation of excited Au NCs moieties and the electron transfer to GQDs. As a result, the photoelectric current of Au NCs/GQDs composite is enhanced by 2.69 and 4.80 times that of individual Au NCs and GQDs, respectively. These findings demonstrate a feasible strategy for constructing photoelectric composites with Au NCs as the sensitizer and carbon-based QDs as the electron acceptor and also provide photophysical insights into the structure-property relationship of Au NCs-based composites.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Band driven efficient electron transfer and photoelectric performance enhancement of gold nanoclusters sensitized graphene quantum dot composites.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Gold nanoclusters (Au NCs) have attracted wide attention due to their ultra-small size, good photostability and biocompatibility, endowing their promising applications in many fields such as sensing [1,2], catalysis [3], imaging [4], and biomedicine science [5,6], etc. Owing to the quantum size and confinement effects, ligand-protected Au NCs have discrete energy levels and exhibit unusual molecule-like electronic structures and optical properties including photoluminescence [7,8], photocatalysis [9], and optical activity [10]. With a deeper understanding of the relationship between the structure and property of fluorescent Au NCs, their potential applications have been increasingly explored in recent years, consisting of multiple-photon excitation imaging, chiral responsive sensing, light harvesting, and light conversion [11–13]. Specifically, Au NCs have become a new member of the sensitizer family for photoelectric conversion and photocatalytic composites. Compared to traditional organic dye molecules, Au NCs have the advantages of resistance to photobleaching and large specific surface area, making them an effective photosensitizing material [14–16]. For example, Chen and coworkers reported that the sensitized Pt/TiO2 nanoparticles with glutathione-capped Au NCs can facilitate the hydrogen generation of photochemical cells and photocatalytic reactors [17]. By sensitizing TiO2 nanotubes with Au NCs, Liu et al. successfully expanded the light-harvesting to the visible region of TiO2 and improved the photochemical characteristics of Au NCs/TiO2 composite [18]. Mondal et al. investigated the photovoltaic and catalytic properties of several nanocomposites comprising CdTe quantum dots and Au NCs with different protected ligands [19]. In addition to the contribution of capping ligands to electron conduction, they observed a photo-induced bidirectional electron transfer in these complexes, in which Au NCs can act as electron donors and also play a role as electron acceptors. A similar function as an electron acceptor was also reported in enhancing the photoelectric properties of Au NCs/CdTe composite [20]. These features endow the potential applications of Au NCs as an outstanding component of photoelectric devices for energy conversion.

In the past decades, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have gained wide attention in electronics, catalysis, photonics, and cross-disciplinary fields due to their advantages of adjustable band-gap, stable structure, low cost, and nontoxic properties [21–26]. Besides the donor functions in energy conversion and photoelectric applications [27,28], GQDs have demonstrated outstanding electron capture capabilities to enhance the photoelectric properties, suggesting their potential as electron acceptors in nanocomposites. For example, Kharkwal et al. reported an efficient interfacial charge transfer from ZnO to GQDs to promote the photoelectric properties of ZnO/GQDs composites, where ZnO was used as light-harvesting moiety to sensitize GQDs and enhance light absorption in UV–visible region [29]. Liu and coworkers constructed a hybrid structure of GQDs/2D-WSe2 and studied the charge transfer from WSe2 to GQDs [30]. They found that the hole carrier mobility can be increased 50 times that of individual WSe2 and an improved electron transport performance. Xiong et al. reported the GQDs-modified Bi2WO6 (BWO) composites and demonstrated the excited electrons can be transferred from the Bi 6p and O 2p orbitals of BWO to the acceptor GQDs, leading to an enhanced photocatalytic property of the composites [31]. Lei et al. prepared SnO2/GQDs composites with the GQDs as the electron acceptor and realized a high photocurrent value of 3.0 × 10−4 A/cm2 [32]. The crucial role of GQDs in improving the optoelectronic properties was also supported by the enhanced photocurrent density of 1.65 × 10−5 A/cm2 for CdSe/GQDs composites, which is 13 times higher than that of CdSe alone [33]. So far, the nanocomposites with GQDs as the electron acceptor are relatively less studied and their photoelectric properties need to be improved for practical application.

Herein, we prepared the Au NCs/GQDs composites with Au NCs as the sensitizer and GQDs as the electron acceptors and investigated their photogenerated carrier dynamics and photoelectric properties using steady-state and time-resolved spectroscopy. The introduction of Au NCs effectively improves the light-harvesting ability in visible spectral regions of Au NCs/GQDs composites. Meanwhile, the presence of GQDs remarkably quenches the fluorescence of Au NCs due to the efficient electron transfer from Au NCs to GQDs. The type-II energy band alignment between the donor Au NCs and acceptor GQDs moieties is greatly conducive to promoting the charge separation, electron transfer, and photoelectric property of Au NCs/GQDs. Accordingly, the photocurrent of the Au NCs/GQDs composite is enhanced by 2.69 and 4.80 times compared to that of Au NCs and GQDs, respectively. These findings demonstrate an effective strategy to develop novel photoelectric composites using metal NCs as the energy donor and carbon-based QDs as the electron acceptor.

The arginine (Arg)-stabilized 6-aza-2-thiothymine (ATT) Au nanoclusters (Arg/ATT-Au NCs) were synthesized by referring to the protocol of literature without adjusting the pH value [34]. Briefly, 5 mL ATT aqueous solution (80 mM) containing 0.2 M NaOH was added into 5 mL HAuCl4 aqueous solution (10 mg/mL) under vigorous stirring in the dark for 60 min at 30 °C. The obtained solution was dialyzed with a dialysis bag (MW: 3.5 kDa) against ultrapure water for 16 h. 1 mL arginine aqueous solution (40 mM) was injected into the resulting ATT-Au NCs solution with a gentle stirring in the dark for 24 h at 37 °C. The fluorescent Arg/ATT-Au NCs solution was obtained and kept at 4 °C in the dark for further use. In this work, we referred the Arg/ATT-Au NCs simply to Au NCs unless otherwise specified.

GQDs were synthesized referring to the procedures of literature with a slight modification [35]. Briefly, a mixture of 1.9 g of tartaric acid and 0.1 g of urea was transferred to a 10 mL PTFE reactor in a stainless steel chamber and heated at 160 °C for 6 h to obtain an orange liquid, indicating the formation of GQDs. The resulting product was then mixed with 100 mL (5 mg/mL) of NaOH solution in a 150 mL beaker and stirred for 15 min. The final solution was freeze-dried to obtain the GQDs powder, which was dispersed into an aqueous solution as needed.

The composites were prepared by mixing 80 μL of Au NCs solution with different amounts of GQDs. The obtained Au NCs/GQDs solutions were further incubated overnight at ambient conditions. The resulting products were diluted as needed and stirred slowly for 2 h for further characterization at room temperature.

A Cary 5000 UV-Vis-NIR spectrophotometer (Agilent) and a Perkin Elmer LS 55 fluorescence spectrometer were employed to capture UV–vis absorption and PL spectra. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were carried out on a JEOL JEM-2100 microscope. A Malvern Zetasizer apparatus was used to measure the zeta-potential (ζ-potential). For the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements, a monochromatic Al Kα source (1486.6 eV) at 100 W was used to capture the valence band spectra on a Thermo Scientific K-AlphaTM+ spectrometer. The crystalline phase measurements were performed on an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, DX-2700) with the monochromatic Cu Ka irradiation (λ = 0.15 nm). The d-spacing value of the sample was obtained by using Bragg's formula of 2dsinθ=nλ, in which d is interplanar spacing, λ is electron wavelength, θ is the diffraction angle of n-order peak in the XRD spectrum. Raman spectra were recorded on a LabRAM Odyssey spectrometer equipped with a 633 nm laser as the excitation source (Horiba Scientific).

Time-resolved fluorescence measurements were performed using a home-built system based on the time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) technique (Harp 300, Picoquant). The samples were excited at 470 nm from a broadband tunable femtosecond Ti:sapphire pulsed laser (Chameleon Ultra II, Coherent, Inc.) with a pulse width of 120 fs after the second harmonic generation of BBO nonlinear crystal. A Pockels cell (Conoptics, Model 305) was used to change the repetition rate of the excitation pulse from 1 MHz to 80 MHz as needed. The emission signals passed through a monochromator and were detected by an MPD detector (Picoquant). The instrumental response function (IRF) was determined to be less than 50 picoseconds. Femtoseconds dynamics were captured on a Helios Fire transient absorption (TA) spectrometer (HELIOS, Ultrafast Systems), combined with a regenerative amplified Ti:sapphire laser system (Astrella Vitara-S, Coherent, Inc.) and an optical parametric amplifier (TOPAS, Coherent, Inc.). The IRF of the TA system is around 140 fs. The dynamic curves obtained from time-resolved fluorescence and femtosecond TA measurements were fitted with the following multi-exponential function convoluted with the corresponding IRF,
(1)
A
t
=
∑
i = 1
n
a
i
exp ( − t /
τ
i
)
where A(t) is the detected signal with time at a selected wavelength, a
i and τ
i are the relative amplitude and the lifetime constant of each component, respectively. The amplitude-averaged lifetime τ
av was used for further analysis.

An ITO-coated glass of 1 × 1 cm was cleaned under ultrasonication for 30 min in acetone, ethanol, and ultrapure water, respectively, and then dried with nitrogen gas. 100 μL of the samples in an aqueous solution were loaded on ITO glass and dried overnight. Photoelectrochemical measurements were performed on an electrochemical analyzer (CHI660E, CHI Instruments) equipped with a 300 W Xe lamp. The photocurrent response of the samples was carried out using linear scanning voltammetry (LSV) with an electrochemical cell consisting of 0.5 M Na2SO4 as the electrolyte, a Pt plate as the counter-electrode and an Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference, respectively. The front side of the working electrode (ITO) faced the standard sunlight during the tests.

TEM and XRD spectra were used to characterize the morphology, size distribution, and crystallinity of as-prepared Au NCs and GQDs. As shown in Fig. 1(a) and (c), Au NCs are spherical with an average size of 1.7 nm and a uniform distribution. The high-resolution (HR) TEM images of Au NCs clearly show a 0.23 nm lattice fringe. The XRD spectrum of Au NCs exhibits a main peak around 17.63° corresponding to a lattice spacing of 0.25 nm (Fig. S1(a)). The discrepancy in d-spacing values obtained from TEM and XRD might originate from the heterogeneity of Au NCs together with the errors between different characterizing methods and insufficient statistics of data. Fig. 1(b) and (c) depict the TEM and HRTEM images as well as the size distribution of GQDs. Similarly, GQDs are also spherical with an average size of 4.87 nm and a lattice fringe of 0.24 nm, reflecting the graphene lattice fringes of (1120) [36]. The XRD spectrum of GQDs powder shows a relatively broad diffraction peak with a center angle of about 17.12° and a lattice spacing of 0.26 nm (Fig. S1(b)). Moreover, the Raman spectrum of GQDs exhibits the typical graphitic features including a D-band around 1405 cm−1 associated with the defects or edges of graphene, and a G-band around 1597 cm−1 attributed to the in-plane stretching mode of sp2 carbon atoms (Fig. S2). The observed higher intensity of the D-peak than the G-peak suggests a high edge roughness or defects ratio and relatively low crystallinity of the as-prepared GQDs.

Fig. 1(d) gives the steady-state absorption and fluorescence spectra of Au NCs and GQDs in an aqueous solution. It can be noticed that the absorption spectrum of GQDs shows the absorption characteristics of traditional semiconductor nanomaterials, demonstrating a dominant UV absorption of π − π * transition from the carbon core and a weak absorption tail in the visible spectral region shorter than 520 nm from the surface defects [37]. Under the excitation at 380 nm, GQDs exhibit a weak and broadband emission from 400 nm to 700 nm with an intensity maximum of 460 nm. As a striking contrast, the absorption spectrum of Au NCs exhibits relatively strong absorption in this spectral region and shows molecule-like features with three absorption bands at ∼404 nm, ∼460 nm, and ∼507 nm. Correspondingly, the as-prepared Au NCs in an aqueous solution have a narrow and strong fluorescence centered at 530 nm. The quantum yield can reach 52% independent of the excitation wavelength [11]. Therefore, it can be expected that the hybridizing of Au NCs and GQDs could enhance the light-harvesting ability and photoelectric performance of Au NCs/GQDs composites, supposing the photoinduced carriers could be effectively transferred between these two moieties.

As we know, the matching of energy levels is crucial to the photoelectric composites comprising the electron donor and acceptor moieties. To assess the possibility of carrier transfer occurring in the Au NCs/GQDs composite, we analyzed their relative band locations of Au NCs and GQDs. According to the Tauc plots from their UV–vis spectra, the bandgap energies of 2.37 eV for Au NCs and 3.10 eV for GQDs were determined ( Fig. 2(a)) [20,38]. By using the valence band X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, their relative energies of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of Au NCs and the valence band maximum (VBM) of GQDs were obtained to be 0.68 eV and 2.35 eV (Fig. 2(b)), respectively. A schematic diagram of the relative energy levels of Au NCs and GQDs can be depicted in Fig. 2(c). We can notice that the energetic level of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) for Au NCs is about 0.94 eV higher than that of conduction band minimum (CBM) for GQDs, demonstrating a characteristic feature of type-II band configuration for the Au NCs/GQDs composite. Therefore, a scientific scenario can be expected that under light excitation, the generated electrons of Au NCs will be transferred to the conduction band of acceptor GQDs, leading to an improved photoelectric property of Au NCs/GQDs composite.

The features of Au NCs as the sensitizer in Au NCs/GQDs composites were investigated by monitoring their steady-state absorption and fluorescence spectral evolutions with changing the concentration of acceptor GQDs. As shown in Fig. 3(a), the spectral profiles remain similar and the absorbance is increased as increasing the concentration of GQDs. This indicates that the introduction of Au NCs effectively improves the light-harvesting ability in the visible region of Au NCs/GQDs composite compared with that of GQDs alone. No apparent spectral shifting or new absorption band of the composites even with excess GQDs suggests the absence of ground-state interaction between Au NCs and GQDs [37]. In sharp contrast, the Au NCs alone show a strong fluorescence around 530 nm under the excitation at 470 nm. However, this fluorescence is dramatically quenched with the presence of GQDs (Fig. 3(b)), indicating a strong interaction between the excited states of Au NCs to GQDs in the composite [39,40]. To quantitatively determine this quenching effect of GQDs, we measured the fluorescence of Au NCs moiety in the Au NCs/GQDs composites while keeping the concentration of Au NCs constant and altering the concentration of GQDs. When the amount of GQDs is increased to 0.3 mg/mL, the fluorescence of Au NCs is not further decreased, suggesting a saturated absorption of Au NCs on GQDs and the most efficient electron transfer from Au NCs to GQDs at this condition. We can estimate the quenching efficiency by comparing the integrated fluorescence intensity of Au NCs and Au NCs/GQDs composites. A similar trend of efficiency is illustrated in Fig. S3 with increasing the concentration of GQDs, and a maximal value of 95% is realized for the situation of 0.3 mg/mL GQDs. It should be mentioned that before the construction of Au NCs/GQDs composites and conducting the measurements of kinetics and photoelectric properties, we compared the quenching effect of GQDs with/without dialysis on the steady-state fluorescence of Au NCs. The results of GQDs after dialysis indicate that the fluorescence quenching efficiency of Au NCs can reach 91%, suggesting a very small effect of the impurities in GQDs preparation on the interaction between Au NCs and GQDs (Fig. S4).

To understand the electron transfer between Au NCs and GQDs, we conducted the time-resolved fluorescence measurements on the Au NCs/GQDs composites by selectively probing the emissions of Au NCs and GQDs moieties. The fluorescence lifetime profiles of Au NCs at 530 nm with 470 nm excitation are shown in Fig. 3(c), and the corresponding fitting parameters are listed in Table 1. Exponentially fitting the fluorescence decay of Au NCs gives two components with an average lifetime of τ0 = 17.00 ns, mainly originating from the ligand-metal charge transfer LMCT [41–45]. Consistent with the evolution in steady-state fluorescence spectra, the average lifetime of Au NCs in the composites is continuously decreased to an almost unchanged value of τ = 3.86 ns with increasing the amount of GQDs from 0 to 0.3 mg/mL. Similarly, we can calculate the electron transfer efficiency via the excited state of Au NCs to the conduction band of GQDs by the formula of Φ = 1-τ/τ0 to be 77% [46], which is consistent with the quenching efficiency of fluorescence. In contrast, there is no change observed in the fluorescence decay curves at 460 nm with varying concentrations of Au NCs (Fig. 3(d)). This indicates no proton transfer occurring from GQDs to Au NCs, confirming the sensitizing function of Au NCs in the Au NCs/GQDs composites. These observations demonstrate the photoexcited electron of Au NCs as the donor can be efficiently transferred to GQDs as the electron acceptor in the composites, which is consistent with the scenario illustrated in Fig. 2. In addition, the ζ-potential measurements on Au NCs and GQDs in an aqueous solution were carried out to deduce the driving force for the formation of Au NCs/GQDs composites. As shown in Fig. S5, the surfaces of both GQDs and Au NCs are negatively charged with different potentials. With the host-guest interaction between Arg and ATT to reduce the vibrational and rotational relaxation of the ATT ligand, the fluorescence of Au NCs is significantly enhanced [34]. As a consequence, the carboxyl group with a negative charge of Arg dominates the ligand-shell surface of Au NCs, resulting in a negative potential of 48 mV [11]. Likewise, there are several possible groups bonded to the surface of GQDs such as −NH2, −OH, and −COOH, which contribute to the less negative potential of 25 mV [37]. Therefore, we speculate that the non-covalent molecular interactions between the surface groups of Au NCs and GQDs would provide the driving force for the proximity of Au NCs and GQDs moieties to form the composites [20].

Femtosecond TA measurements were further carried out to probe the ultrafast electron transfer from Au NCs to GQDs moieties in the Au NCs/GQDs composites. As shown in Fig. 4 and Fig. S6, under a pump excitation at 470 nm, a broadband negative signal around 513 nm dominates the TA spectra of Au NCs alone and Au NCs/GQDs composite (0.3 mg/mL). Combined with the steady-state absorption and fluorescence spectral evolution with changing the amounts of GQDs, we attribute this negative TA signal mainly to the ground-state bleaching (GSB) of Au NCs moiety, although the contribution of stimulated-emission (SE) is probably merged in this spectral region [47,48]. Due to the molecular-like electronic structure and excitation-independent fluorescence of Au NCs (Fig. S7), either the recovery of GSB or the decay of SE can reflect the electron deactivation from the lowest excited state to the ground state. The GSB signal of Au NCs in Au NCs/GQDs composite decays is faster than that of Au NCs alone, consistent with the observed changes in the fluorescence lifetime. Fig. 4(d) and Fig. S6(d) show the extracted GSB recovery kinetics at 513 nm of individual Au NCs and Au NCs/GQDs composites. Their fitting parameters including the average lifetime are summarized in Table 2.

Specifically, the average time constant of the GSB recovery process of Au NCs is significantly decreased with the presence of GQDs, which is changed from 2.91 ns for Au NCs alone to 0.80 ns for Au NCs/GQDs (0.3 mg/mL). The observed ultrafast component with tens of picoseconds suggests an additional electron transfer pathway, which is not well-resolved in the fluorescence lifetime. Likewise, the electron transfer efficiency of 73% from Au NCs to GQDs in the composite can be obtained, which is consistent with the efficiency from the analysis of fluorescence dynamics. It is worth mentioning that the contribution from the long lifetime component of tens of nanoseconds is hard to resolve due to the limitation of the time delay window in the femtosecond TA system. Meanwhile, we can notice that the amplitude of the GSB signal for the Au NCs/GQDs composite is smaller than that of individual Au NCs at the initial time delay. Since the amplitude of the TA signal is proportional to the carrier density, we infer that the decreased GSB signal at an earlier delay time of Au NCs/GQDs composite, compared with that of Au NCs, could originate from the additional ultrafast charge/electron transfer processes even to sub-picosecond occurring between Au NCs and GQDs moieties [20,49,50], leading to a slight discrepancy between the high quenching efficiency in steady-state fluorescence and the relatively low efficiency determined from the time-resolved dynamics. On the other hand, the diversity of electron transfer components in the time-resolved fluorescence and TA also reflects the heterogeneous interactions between Au NCs and GQDs, which could be associated with their surface groups and binding modes. Based on the alignment of energetic levels, the consistent observations in steady-state spectra and time-resolved dynamics, we can conclude that the efficient electron transfer from the donor Au NCs to the acceptor GQDs is responsible for the high fluorescence quenching of Au NCs/GQDs composites, which can be expected to improve their photoelectric properties.

To evaluate the photoelectric properties of Au NCs, GQDs and Au NCs/GQDs (0.3 mg/mL), we performed the electrochemical measurements with a three-electrode cell system shown in Fig. 5(a). The Au NCs/GQDs film on the ITO substrate was used as the working electrode with the reference electrode of Ag/AgCl and the counter electrode Pt sheet for photocurrent measurements [51]. Fig. 5(b) shows LSV photocurrent response with the applied bias voltage under dark and simulated daylight irradiation in 0.5 M Na2SO4 electrolyte. Compared to a value close to zero in the dark, the photocurrent density under the simulated solar light irradiation shows an obvious photoelectric response for Au NCs, GQDs and Au NCs/GQDs composite. Specifically, individual Au NCs and GQDs exhibit a slow increase in the photocurrent density with changing the bias voltage from 0.7 to 1.05 V, reflecting their intrinsic weak photoelectric conversion due to the direct recombination of photogenerated carriers via radiative and non-radiative processes. As a striking contrast, the photocurrent of Au NCs/GQDs composite demonstrates a substantial and sharp increase relative to that of Au NCs or GQDs. At 1.0 V of bias voltage, the photocurrent density of Au NCs/GQDs composite can reach ∼814.15 nA cm−2 under standard sunlight irradiation, which is 2.69 times that of 302.60 nA cm−2 for Au NCs and 4.80 times that of 169.45 nA cm−2 for GQDs. This significant enhancement can be attributed to the efficient electron transfer from Au NCs to GQDs moieties in the composite. The effective charge separation of Au NCs dramatically inhibits the direction recombination of photogenerated carriers and promotes the photoelectric conversion of Au NCs/GQDs composite. Fig. 5(c) depicts the photocurrent response of Au NCs, GQDs and Au NCs/GQDs under stimulated sunlight irradiation at a given bias of 1.0 V. It can be noticed that the photocurrent drops to zero after turning off the light irradiation and shows a reversible feature for these three samples. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) results also support the enhanced photoelectric property of Au NCs/GQDs composite (Fig. 5(d)). Compared to that of Au NCs and GQDs, the relatively smaller radius of EIS for the composite indicates a decreased carrier resistance, suggesting the interfacial carrier transportation between the composite film and the electrolyte is improved to facilitate the electron transfer between the Au NCs and GQDs moieties. Therefore, the synergy of matching in energy level, efficient electron transfer and complementary light absorption between the donor Au NCs and the acceptor GQDs endows the enhanced photoelectric property of Au NCs/GQDs composite.

In summary, we have successfully constructed the Au NCs/GQDs composites by sensitizing GQDs with Au NCs and investigated the enhanced photoelectric properties and electron transfer dynamics. Analyzing the energetic levels of two moieties indicates a type-II band alignment between Au NCs and GQDs, promoting the carrier transfer and photoelectric conversion of the composite. We suggest that the non-covalent intermolecular interactions between the surface groups of two moieties facilitate the formation of Au NCs/GQDs composite. The introduction of Au NCs significantly makes up for the deficiency of light absorption of GQDs in the visible light region. Compared to a strong fluorescence of individual Au NCs, the Au NCs/GQDs composite exhibits a dramatically quenched emission of Au NCs moiety, indicating the typical characteristics of Au NCs as the sensitizer. Time-resolved fluorescence and absorption dynamics demonstrate the photogenerated electron of Au NCs can be efficiently transferred to the electron acceptor GQDs. The electron transfer efficiency determined from the dynamical analysis shows good consistency with that of steady-state fluorescence quenching. Accordingly, the synergetic effect of the complementary in light absorption, type-II energy band alignment, and efficient electron transfer from between Au NCs and GQDs moieties significantly improve the photoelectric conversion of Au NCs/GQDs composite, compared to that of either individual Au NCs or GQDs. These findings demonstrate a feasible strategy for constructing photoelectric composites with Au NCs as the electron donor and carbon-based nanoparticles as the electron acceptor, and also provide more insights into the photophysical mechanism of enhanced photoelectric properties.

Guangjing Hou: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Yatao Pan: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Visualization. Lulu Qiao: Investigation, Data curation, Validation. Xia Ran: Conceptualization, Writing − review & editing. Tianfeng Li: Data curation, Validation. Zhen Chi: Methodology, Formal analysis, Validation, Visualization. Pingan Liu: Resources, Validation. Yulu He: Resources, Data curation. Yanmin Kuang: Methodology, Formal analysis. Lijun Guo: Supervision, Project administration, Writing − review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (project No. 22105063, 22103024, 12004101, and 61905066). The Science and Technology Development Project of Henan Province (222102210217). The Key Scientific Research Project of Colleges and Universities in Henan Province (Project no. 23A510013).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2023.172261.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
